items.q: Why does plastic accumulate in ocean gyres?
items.correct: Gyres are large rotating ocean currents driven by wind and Coriolis effect; floating debris is carried in but the rotation traps it in the centre, where currents are weak, concentrating plastic over decades.
items.misconception: The 'Great Pacific Garbage Patch' is a solid floating island of plastic you can walk on.
items.random_wrong: Plastic accumulates in gyres because the water there is denser.
items.why: The patch is mostly invisible microplastic suspended in the water column, not a continent. The trapping mechanism is fluid-dynamic — Ekman transport into a centre with weak outflow.

items.correct: Longshore drift carries sediment past a change in coastline direction (e.g. river mouth); deposition continues into deeper water, building a curved depositional landform with a hook.
items.misconception: A spit forms where the river deposits its sediment at the coast.
items.random_wrong: A spit is a small island just off the coast.
items.why: Spits are coastal (longshore drift) deposits, not river deposits. The hook forms where wind shifts the current. Students confuse spits with deltas — different processes, different settings.

items.q: Why does oceanic crust subduct beneath continental crust?
items.correct: Oceanic crust is denser (basaltic) than continental crust (granitic), so when they collide the denser oceanic plate sinks into the mantle, forming a trench and volcanic arc.
items.misconception: Oceanic crust subducts because the ocean pushes it down with its weight.
items.random_wrong: Continental crust subducts because it is thicker and heavier.
items.why: Density, not water pressure, drives subduction. Continental crust is too buoyant to subduct, which is why continent-continent collisions produce fold mountains instead of trenches.

items.q: Which African country has the largest population?
items.why: Correct answer: Nigeria. “Kenya” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Safaris and wildlife. “Pyramids” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Nigeria's population is roughly how many million?
items.why: The real figure is 200. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
